text: The laparoscopic view shows the gallbladder positioned at the base of the liver. The surgeon performs dissection in the Calot's triangle, aiming to separate the cystic duct and cystic artery.

During the laparoscopic cholecystectomy, the surgeon uses a four-port approach to separate the cystic duct, applying three clips to secure it. Two clips remain inside the body, while one will be removed with the gallbladder. The surgeon then clips the cystic artery, creating space for the procedure. A visible lymph node is identified, with the first clip placed on it, and the white structure above is confirmed as the lymph node.
The surgeon cuts the cystic duct and begins dissecting the gallbladder from the cystic plate using a Hickory device. A slight bile leakage is visible during the dissection process.

The surgeon removes the gallbladder using an ovum forceps, extracting stones from it. The surgical field is irrigated, a drain is placed, and the pneumoperitoneum is deflated. The alpha port incisions are stretched back.
